w:r.w:t: Design · Copywriting · Code · SEO/AEO · Performance · Conversion · Security · Post-Launch
March 2026 · All 9 Source Documents + 30 Gap Fixes Consolidated
Resend, design trends, color palette, SaaS strategy, AI workflows
Typography (desktop/mobile/H1–H6), 8px grid, color system + dark mode, image specs
Homepage 8-section layout, 15-element blog formula, scroll effects + fallbacks
Schema markup, AEO/GEO checklist, E-E-A-T, canonical, OG tags, robots.txt, sitemap, multilingual hreflang, CMS integration (WordPress, Contentful, Webflow, Shopify, Ghost, Sanity)
50+ headlines (5 formulas), 130+ CTAs, body copy frameworks, power words, pricing pages
CSS tokens + dark mode, HTML templates (homepage + blog + pricing page), animations + fallbacks, favicon, auth security
Targets, tech stack, image strategy, video embeds, monitoring, performance budgets
Above-fold, CTAs, trust elements, forms, A/B framework, pricing page structure
HTTPS/HSTS, CSP + nonce guidance, form security, auth/password security, GDPR + working cookie consent JS, cookie libraries, prefers-reduced-motion, WCAG, 404 guidance
10-step page build, 6-phase launch, 40-item checklist, ongoing cadence calendar
This section explains WHY certain approaches work — the market forces, company case studies, design trends, and strategic shifts that inform every specification in Parts 2–10.
Human-Centric Design Revolution: After years of algorithmic sameness, designers are reclaiming taste, craft, and authenticity. The web is becoming slower, smaller, and infinitely more human.
Minimalist Developer-First Approach Dominates: Resend proves clean, focused design with exceptional developer experience outperforms flashy visuals. Clarity trumps complexity.

Zero-Click Search Era: 58–60% of searches end without site visits, but AI-driven traffic converts 27% higher. Success requires dual optimization for both traditional SEO and Answer Engine Optimization (AEO).
Performance as Foundation: Sites achieving Core Web Vitals pass rates see 18% lower bounce rates. Performance is no longer optional — it is the baseline for everything else.
AI-Native Design Workflows: Leading teams like Notion use Claude Code for rapid prototyping. Design and development are merging productively.
Color Trends Reflect Emotional Intelligence: Pantone's Cloud Dancer (serene off-white) and WGSN's Transformative Teal represent the market's desire for calm amid chaos.

Machine Experience (MX) Design: Websites are now built for both humans AND AI agents that read, interpret, and summarize content. Semantic HTML, clear IA, and structured data are critical for both audiences.
1.2 The Resend Phenomenon — Why Minimalism Wins

Resend is the definitive case study in how minimalist design philosophy combined with exceptional execution creates market-leading products (1M+ users, 5-minute setup, no credit card required at signup).
Design for the Margins, Not the Center:

Design for the 90th percentile user — older hardware, slower internet, less popular languages. This forces faster load times, generous free tiers, SDKs for all languages, and beginner-friendly documentation.
No credit card, no multi-step verification, no demo calls at signup. Instant value, immediate clarity, zero friction. If the first attempt takes hours, users won't return.
Docs have CI/CD, live in-app, follow a roadmap. Beautiful documentation is infrastructure, not a nice-to-have.
Uptime is as essential as water for life. Every downtime erodes trust permanently.
Clean code examples, React Email, TypeScript-first, beautiful docs
Precise typography, minimal interface, intentional animations
Preferred over Jira for performance teams
Gradient animations, clear code snippets, product visualization
Dark mode aesthetic, instant deployment, Next.js optimization
Figma→code via Claude Code, shared prototype playground
80% complete components in minutes vs hours
1.3 The Restoration Era — March 2026 Design Trends

March 2026 marks a return to intentionality. After years of chasing automation, designers are seeking meaning, intention, and the human fingerprint that got lost along the way. This isn't revolutionary — it's restorative.
Blank canvas, dot grid, node lines, expansive space — deliberate symbolism communicating 'this is where creation happens.' Anthropic's website exemplifies this.
Hand-drawn elements, ASCII art, classical art references combined with modern UI. Pencil.dev uses ASCII figures alongside contemporary web functionality.
Users arrive with specific jobs to be done. Buttons remain invisible until behavior indicates need. Forms auto-save and smart-resume.
Interfaces adapt pacing and simplify paths in real-time. Netflix shifts thumbnails based on behavioral cues (time of day, hesitation duration) — a driver of 15–16% YoY revenue growth.
1.4 Color Trends March 2026 — The Complete Palette
Pantone Color of the Year 2026: Cloud Dancer

Pantone 11-4201 'Cloud Dancer' — a soft, airy off-white between subtle warm and cool tones. Radiates calm and clarity. Adopted by Bottega Veneta, Balenciaga, Jil Sander, Celine, and Carven for Spring/Summer 2026. Hex equivalent: #F5F5F0 (implementation value throughout this document).

WGSN/Coloro reference: Coloro 036-27-14. A deep blue-green symbolizing regeneration, sustainability, and forward-looking attitude. Hex implementation value: #0D7377. Note: the exact WGSN/Coloro proprietary swatch is a licensed commercial reference — #0D7377 is the closest publicly available hex match to the described hue. Use your brand's specific approved hex if you have access to the WGSN swatch library.

This document uses #0D7377 as the Transformative Teal implementation value. All code examples have been updated to use this value. Replace with your exact brand hex if you have the official WGSN/Coloro swatch licensed.
Primary backgrounds, large surfaces, calm base
Pure white surfaces, cards, content areas
Subtle accent, nature connection, secondary surfaces
Deep blue-purple, sophisticated accent, hover states
Maximum energy, urgent CTAs, sale/alert states
Maximum 2–3 colors (base + 1–2 accents). WCAG AAA 7:1 contrast minimum for body text. Never pure #000000 on pure #FFFFFF. Test every combination with WebAIM Contrast Checker before implementation.
1.5 SaaS Conversion Strategy — What Works in March 2026
If your site doesn't explain what you do in 3–5 seconds, users leave and don't come back. They're comparing 5 tools, 6 tabs open. No patience.
Five Critical Success Factors with Proven Data
6-word hero headline → 27% more demo clicks in 9 days. Same product. Same design. Just clarity. One message → one CTA → one next step.
Social proof above the fold — logos, numbers, screenshots. Highrise: +102.5% signups by adding photos to testimonials.
Mix UI with real people, real environments. Screens alone don't sell. Humanity sells.
Micro animations showing how something works beat cinematic animations that slow the page. Speed = trust.
Interactive demos > illustrations. Tolstoy: replacing generic animations with interactive shoppable video doubled demo requests.
Added real customer photos to testimonials

Brian Lovin built a shared AI-powered prototype playground letting the entire design team turn Figma designs into working Next.js code using Claude Code. The /figma command: (1) Extract design tokens via Figma API, (2) Implement in Next.js + Tailwind, (3) Verify in browser via Chrome DevTools MCP, (4) Auto-fix issues. Result: complex sidebar components 80% complete in minutes vs. hours.
Anytime the AI asks you to do something, before responding, try your best to see if you could teach the AI to answer that question for itself.

Mario Braz (finance background, zero engineering training) used v0 to build a full financial modelling application on a single flight. Result: 92 weekly users vs. 23 in the old Google Sheets model — 288% increase in adoption. One person, one flight, months of traditional development time eliminated.

Every measurement here is implementation-ready. Derived from analysis of top-performing websites, March 2026 market research, and Google/Bing compliance requirements. Fixes: H3–H6 defined for mobile, dark mode specifications added, tablet breakpoint defined.
Apply these specifications to all elements viewed on screens wider than 1140px. Use the lower value of each range for body content pages; use the upper value for bold landing pages and hero sections.
ONE H1 per page — mandatory, no exceptions
Children of H3. Use for deep-nested content.
Use sparingly — most pages need H1–H4 only
Rarely needed — use for sidebar labels, table captions
Mobile typography prioritises readability on small viewports and touch usability. Line heights are slightly higher than desktop to aid scanning on small screens.
Ensure visibility above fold. Must dominate viewport.
Clear hierarchy essential on small screen
Distinguish clearly from H2 and body text
Must be visually distinct from body — use bold weight
Keep consistent with body; differentiate via bold + color
Minimum size — use only when absolutely necessary
Tablet is a commonly missed breakpoint. Apply these intermediate values between mobile and desktop specs:
Larger than mobile, smaller than desktop
Same as desktop — comfortable on tablet viewports
ALL spacing MUST follow 8px rhythm. Every margin, padding, and gap must be a multiple of 8. This creates visual consistency across every element on every screen size.
45–85 characters per line. Optimal: 65 characters. NEVER full-width text. CSS: max-width: 65ch on all paragraph elements.
Text stretched full-screen = immediate readability failure
Every color in this system is named for its semantic role, not its appearance. Use the token names throughout your CSS rather than hardcoded hex values — this makes dark mode and rebranding trivial.
All body text on light backgrounds
Supporting text, headings on white bg
CTAs, links, nav highlights, focus rings
Error states, urgent CTAs, sale flags

Dark mode is a baseline expectation in 2026. 82% of smartphone users prefer dark mode. This is not optional for any new website. Implement via CSS @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) and a manual toggle. CSS custom properties (design tokens) make this trivial — see Part 6.1.
These values define the structural dimensions of every major layout container. Apply them consistently across all pages to ensure visual coherence between desktop, tablet, and mobile viewports.
Image dimensions and file sizes are not suggestions — they are performance budgets. Every image above the fold that exceeds these targets directly degrades LCP and conversion rate.

Exact placement for every element — homepage, blog, images, and scroll effects. The blog formula has been updated from 14 to 15 elements (Conclusion and Author Bio are separate elements). Scroll effects include browser fallbacks.
3.1 Homepage Structure (8 Sections, Top to Bottom)

Every section below is required. Removing any one of the 8 elements — especially the social proof bar or the CTA section — measurably reduces conversion rate. The order is based on the psychological journey a visitor takes from first awareness to decision.
H1 (6–10 words) + Subheadline (1–2 sentences) + Primary CTA + Hero image RIGHT 50% + Optional social proof logos
'Trusted by' + 4–6 company logos OR key metric ('10,000+ teams')
H2 + 3–4 feature cards (Icon + Title + 2–3 sentences each) + 32px gaps
H2 + Product screenshot LEFT 50% + Feature list RIGHT + Secondary CTA
H2 + 2–3 cards (60–80px photo + Name + Company + Quote 3–4 sentences)
H2 + Brief desc (1–2 sentences) + Large CTA button + 'No credit card required'
Company info + links (3–4 columns) + Social icons + Copyright + Legal links
AI engines read top-down. Structure must follow this exact sequence. Elements 14 (Conclusion) and 15 (Author Bio) are separate — the formula has 15 elements, not 14.
Author + date + reading time + optional tags | 14–16px gray (#666666)
40–60 words, direct answer in highlighted box | Light bg, 24px padding, max-width 700px
For posts >1000 words — clickable anchors to all H2s | Bordered box, 16px padding
Context + keyword in first 100 words | 17–18px, max-width 700px
Each H2 = searchable question | 2–4 paras per section | Image every 300–500 words
H2: 'Frequently Asked Questions' | 5–10 Q&A | Bold Q, regular A, 24px between | Each A: 40–60 words
2–3 paras summarizing + CTA to related content | Standard body, max-width 700px
Photo (80px circle) + Name + Title + 2–3 sentence bio + social links

Word count targets are informed by what currently ranks, not arbitrary minimums. Quality always beats length — a 900-word article that fully answers the question outperforms a 2,500-word article padded with filler.
Average top-ranking content: 1,447 words. Target 1,500+ to exceed average and cover topic fully.
Authority-building — cover every sub-question a reader might have
Only if the question is fully answered at this length
Detailed analysis required — include data, methodology, results

The '1,447 words' average (cited in research) is the current average for top-ranking content — not the target. Target 1,500–2,500 to exceed the baseline. Quality always beats quantity: cover the topic completely with zero filler.
2–4 sentences maximum, single idea per paragraph
One every 300–500 words — articles with images get 94% more views
Never create walls of text — break up every 3–4 paragraphs
Highlight key points only — overuse destroys emphasis
3–5 per page with descriptive anchor text

Image placement follows both reader psychology and SEO logic. Images break up text (improving dwell time), illustrate concepts, and signal to Google that the content is comprehensive. Place them at the positions below for maximum impact.
Grab attention, set topic, OG share image

Different images serve different cognitive purposes. Screenshots build trust by showing the real product. Diagrams explain processes. Infographics get shared. Use the right type for the right context — not all images are interchangeable.
Immediately after the step description — always centered
After data section, before analysis — center, allow click to expand
Hero (right 50%) OR left/right 50% split with text opposite
Between sections — use very sparingly, prefer original content
Above the video — see Part 7.4 for video embed performance rules
Do not overuse scroll effects. Strategic placement only. Every effect must have a prefers-reduced-motion fallback (see Part 9.3).
Content images as they enter viewport, feature cards, testimonials, section headings
Hero section background, large showcase images
Blog content images — LIMIT to 1–2 sections MAX
Feature showcase images, portfolio items, product images
Long blog posts (>1000 words), long-form sales pages
CSS scroll() timeline, fixed top bar, 2–4px height

Fixes in this version: GEO defined and distinguished from AEO, canonical URL strategy added, Open Graph/Twitter Card meta tags added, robots.txt/sitemap guidance added, internal linking strategy expanded.

In 2026, visibility requires optimising for three distinct channels simultaneously. Traditional SEO (Google blue links) remains the highest-volume channel, but GEO and AEO are growing rapidly as AI-generated answers displace direct clicks.
Rank high in Google/Bing results to drive clicks
Still essential — 40%+ of traffic for most sites
Be cited within AI-generated answers (Google AI Overviews, Bing Copilot)
CRITICAL — 47% of searches now have AI Overviews
Be sourced by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and Claude when users ask questions directly
Brand mentions in generative AI responses, share of AI voice

AEO = optimizing for AI features within traditional search results (Google AI Overviews). GEO = optimizing for standalone generative AI tools (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Gemini) used outside of Google. Both require: structured content, direct answers, named authors, E-E-A-T signals, and schema markup. GEO additionally rewards: being cited in other authoritative sources, Wikipedia presence, and consistent entity representation across the web.
40–60 word direct answer in the first 540 words. This is the #1 signal for AI grounding.
H2 = 'How do I...?' NOT 'Overview'. Every H2 must be a natural search question.
Answer the question in 2–3 sentences immediately under the H2, then elaborate.
Target full-sentence questions users ask voice assistants and AI chatbots.
Connect your brand to relevant entities consistently. Use identical names across all platforms.
Link to authoritative sources. Be cite-able yourself (get mentioned in reputable publications).
5–10 questions with 40–60 word standalone answers on every blog post.
Faceless content is filtered from AI citations. Named authors with credentials = trust.
Update dateModified in schema on every content edit. AI systems prefer fresh content.
To appear in ChatGPT/Perplexity responses: get mentioned in Wikipedia, Forbes, TechCrunch, or other high-authority publications that these AI systems were trained on.

Google's quality rater guidelines evaluate content on four dimensions: Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trustworthiness. Implement all four — they are signals to both human readers and AI systems evaluating your credibility.
First-person examples, real use cases, 'In my experience...' phrasing, original data and experiment results
Author credentials above fold, professional background, certifications, industry awards visible on every post
Mentions in reputable publications, external backlinks from high-authority domains, case studies with verifiable results
Regular updates with visible dateModified, fact-checking notes, cited sources, transparent methodology, company About page

NOTE: The schema headline field allows up to 110 characters (schema.org spec). Your H1 display title should be 50–60 characters for SEO. The schema headline can be more descriptive than the visible H1.
<!-- NOTE: schema.org headline allows 110 chars. Your H1 display title = 50-60 chars for SEO. -->
"headline": "Article Title Here — can be up to 110 characters for schema",
4.5 Open Graph & Social Meta Tags ✦ NEW

When someone shares your URL on LinkedIn, X, WhatsApp, or Slack, the platform reads og:title, og:image, and og:description to generate the preview card. Without these tags, social shares show no image and use the raw URL. This kills click-through rate from every social share.

og:image MUST be JPEG or PNG — NOT WebP. Many social crawlers (including older LinkedIn and WhatsApp versions) do not support WebP for preview images. Always use .jpg for your OG image even if your site uses WebP everywhere else.
<!-- Open Graph tags (LinkedIn, Facebook, WhatsApp, Slack) -->
<meta property="og:description" content="Same as meta description — 155-160 chars">

Pagination, category pages, tag archives, author pages, and URL parameters all create duplicate content. Without canonical tags, Google may split ranking signals across multiple URLs — or penalize the site for duplicate content. Canonical tags tell Google which URL is the 'master' version.
<!-- Self-referencing canonical on every page (including homepage) -->
<!-- Paginated content: page 2, 3, etc. point back to page 1 -->
<!-- If content is syndicated to another site, THAT site -->
<!-- should add canonical pointing back to YOUR original URL -->
Every page must have a canonical tag:
including homepage, category pages, and tag archives
a page can canonical to itself
always canonical to the HTTPS version
pick one (with or without) and canonical consistently
canonical to one version, set preferred domain in Search Console

Apply every element below to every page before publishing. Title tag and meta description affect click-through rate directly. Schema markup and internal links affect both rankings and AI engine visibility.
Primary keyword near start. Unique for every page.
Compelling CTA included. Impacts CTR, not rankings.
Self-referencing on every page, every URL
Short, descriptive, keywords separated by hyphens
In first 100 words, H1, URL, and meta description
Include keyword naturally where genuinely relevant
Descriptive anchor text. Avoid 'click here' or 'read more'.
Link to sources that strengthen your argument
Never skip heading levels. Never use headings for styling.
Required for all social sharing previews
Robots.txt controls which pages search engines crawl. Every website needs a correctly configured robots.txt.
# Block admin, auth, and private pages from crawling
Disallow: /*?* # Block URL parameters (optional — use with care)
Disallow: / # Block OpenAI crawler if you prefer
<!-- For large sites, use a sitemap index: -->
Same process via Bing's equivalent dashboard
Next.js: next-sitemap package | Astro: @astrojs/sitemap | WordPress: Yoast/Rank Math
Homepage: 1.0 | Key landing pages: 0.9 | Blog posts: 0.8 | Category pages: 0.6
noindex pages, paginated pages after page 1, thin content pages
'3–5 links per page' is the minimum. Here is the full framework for building a content architecture that compounds over time:
Create 1 comprehensive guide per topic cluster (2,500–5,000 words)
Pillar pages earn the most links and rank for broad keywords
Write 8–15 supporting posts that link TO the pillar page
Cluster posts rank for long-tail keywords; links flow to pillar
Every cluster post links to the pillar; pillar links to all clusters
Creates clear topic authority for Google and AI systems
Use descriptive keyword-rich anchors: 'how to improve page speed' not 'click here'
Anchor text is a ranking signal — make it descriptive
Max 1 internal link per 150–200 words; max 10–15 internal links per page
Too many links dilute link equity and confuse users
Prioritize links to pages you want to rank or convert on
Every internal link passes authority — be intentional
When publishing new posts, go back and add links from older relevant posts
Old posts with authority can boost new content's ranking

If you publish content in more than one language, or target users in specific countries, hreflang tags tell Google which version of a page to serve to which audience. Without them, Google may show Spanish users your English content — or duplicate content penalties may apply.
<!-- In <head> of EVERY language variant of the same page: -->
<!-- x-default: fallback for users with no matching language -->
1. Every page must reference ALL other language versions (including itself)
2. hreflang is bidirectional — both pages must reference each other
3. x-default is required — points to the "default" or language-selector page
4. Use ISO 639-1 language codes (en, es, fr, de) + optional ISO 3166-1 region (en-us, en-gb)
Strongest geo-signal. Requires separate hosting per country.
Clear separation. Easier than ccTLD. Most CMS platforms support this.
Simplest to implement. Shares domain authority. Recommended for most sites.
NOT recommended — Google discourages parameter-based language variants
Google detects auto-translated content and may penalise it. Human review required.
Title tags, meta descriptions, OG tags, schema markup, and alt text must all be in the target language.
Keywords in Spanish are NOT direct translations of English keywords. Research each market separately.
Use local currency symbols, date formats (DD/MM/YYYY vs MM/DD/YYYY), and address formats.

Next.js, Astro, and Qwik represent the modern developer stack, but WordPress, Contentful, Sanity, and Webflow represent the majority of websites being built in 2026. Here is how to apply this system's specifications on each platform.
Yoast SEO or Rank Math (both generate schema, sitemaps, canonical tags automatically)
Yoast/Rank Math handle Article, FAQPage, BreadcrumbList schema via UI
Use ShortPixel, Imagify, or WebP Express plugins for WebP conversion. Install WP Rocket for caching + minification.
Use lightweight theme (GeneratePress, Kadence). Avoid Elementor/Divi page builders — they add 500KB+ JS/CSS bloat.
Manual — implement via Next.js or Astro frontend using this guide's SEO section
Add schema via frontend template — Contentful stores content only
Pairs with Next.js or Astro frontend. Full control over performance.
Manual — same as Contentful. Use sanity.io/plugins for structured data helpers
Excellent for structured content modelling. Pairs well with Next.js.
Built-in SEO fields for title, description, OG tags. Add custom schema via embed blocks.
Add JSON-LD via Webflow Embed component in <head>
Built-in WebP conversion. Use Webflow's built-in image optimization settings.
Generally good performance. Avoid adding too many custom JS libraries.
Built-in: canonical, OG, Twitter cards, Article schema auto-generated
Ghost generates Article schema automatically — add FAQPage via code injection
Built-in image optimization and lazy loading
Extremely fast by default. One of the best-performing CMS platforms available.
Built-in basic SEO. Add JSON-LD via theme.liquid or app (Schema Plus for SEO)
Use Schema Plus for SEO app for full schema markup control
Built-in CDN. Use .webp extension in Liquid templates: {{ image | img_url: '1200x' }}
Good default performance. Avoid installing too many apps — each adds JS overhead.

'Sign up' → 'Start free trial' = 104% conversion increase | 6-word hero = 27% more demo clicks in 9 days | Reducing form fields 11→4 = 120% conversion increase | First-person CTA ('Start My Trial') often outperforms second-person
Rules: 50–60 characters | Question format for AEO | ONE H1 per page
How to Double Your Traffic in 30 Days
Why [Problem] Happens & How to Fix It
Why Your Website Loads Slowly & How to Fix It
7 Ways to Boost Your Email Open Rates
The Complete Guide to SEO in 2026
What Google Won't Tell You About Core Web Vitals
How I Got 10,000 Users in 3 Months
5 Mistakes That Are Killing Your Conversions
The 30-Day Plan to Launch Your SaaS
PAS headlines work by naming the reader's pain, intensifying it, then presenting a solution. Highest-performing formula for audiences who know they have a problem but haven't committed to solving it.
Are You Making These 7 SEO Mistakes?
Why [Negative Result] Happens (And How to Fix It)
Why Your Ads Fail (And How to Fix It)
Low Conversions? Here's What You're Doing Wrong
The Truth About [Topic] Nobody Tells You
The Truth About Email Marketing Nobody Tells You
5 Reasons Why Your Content Isn't Ranking
Struggling with Lead Gen? Try This Instead
How to Stop Cart Abandonment Forever
Your [Topic] Is Broken. Here's How to Fix It
Your Sales Funnel Is Broken. Here's How to Fix It
Fed Up with [Problem]? Here's the Answer
Fed Up with Low Traffic? Here's the Answer

BAB creates desire by showing the gap between where the reader is now (before) and where they want to be (after), then positioning your content or product as the bridge. Effective for transformation-focused content.
From [Bad State] to [Good State]: Here's How
From 0 to 10,000 Monthly Visitors: Here's How
What If You Could Rank #1 Without Buying Backlinks?
Imagine 30% More Conversions. Here's the Path
Before: High Bounce. After: Engaged. Difference: Better UX
Stop Losing Leads. Start Converting Them.

4Ps (Problem-Picture-Proof-Proposal) and 4Us (Urgent-Unique-Useful-Ultra-specific) are frameworks for structuring persuasive headlines that are both specific and credible. Combine with AIDA or PAS for maximum effect.
Losing Traffic? Imagine 10x Growth. We Did It. Here's the Method.
How [Proof: Company] Solved [Problem] (And You Can Too)
How Stripe Doubled Conversions (And You Can Too)
47 Ways to Cut Page Load in Half This Week
The Only [Topic] Guide You'll Ever Need
The Only Core Web Vitals Checklist You'll Ever Need
Rank Page 1 by This Time Next Month
'Start My Free Trial' beats 'Sign Up' by 104%
See It In Action (No Sales Call)
Start Free — No Credit Card Required

A value proposition is the single clearest statement of who you help, what outcome they get, and why you over alternatives. Every H1, hero subheadline, and ad needs one. Use the templates below — fill in the brackets with specifics.
Email delivery for developers without configuration headaches
Platform that helps SaaS teams reduce churn by 40%
Launch your store in 24 hours — no code required
The fastest way to build React applications
For [target] who [problem], our [solution] provides [benefit] — unlike [alt], we [differentiator]
For devs who need reliable email, Resend provides instant delivery — unlike Sendgrid, we designed for developer experience first
State what it does: 'Our platform processes payments in real-time'
What that means: 'So you never have delays in your checkout flow'
How it changes their life: 'Meaning you capture every sale, even during peak traffic'
Define the painful problem in the reader's own words
Agitate the consequences of not solving it
Tell the story of someone who had this problem and solved it
Paint the vivid picture of life after the solution
Present your specific offer with all its components
Clear call to action — make it easy to say yes
Ranking #1 in 30 days is possible. Here's proof.
What if your website sold for you while you slept?
68% of websites are losing conversions due to this one mistake.
If you've ever [painful experience], you know how [emotion] that feels.
If you've ever watched ad spend drain with no results, you know how frustrating that feels.
Everyone says you need 10,000 backlinks to rank. They're wrong.
Now, Today, Immediately, Instantly, Limited, Deadline, Last chance, This week only, Act now
CTAs, time-sensitive offers, prompting immediate action
Proven, Guaranteed, Certified, Verified, Secure, Trusted, Endorsed, Authentic, Backed, Validated
Near forms, pricing sections, security badges, testimonials
Warning, Mistake, Risk, Danger, Avoid, Stop, Never, Costly, Losing, Missing out
Problem sections, agitate phase of PAS, risk-reversal copy
Easy, Fast, Simple, Free, Effortless, Powerful, Effective, Better, Smarter, Exclusive
Feature bullets, benefit descriptions, CTA buttons
Secret, Hidden, Revealed, Discover, Uncover, What nobody tells you, The real reason
Used by, Trusted by, Join [N], As seen in, Award-winning, Industry-leading
Transform, Revolutionize, Double, Triple, 10x, Breakthrough, Game-changer, Unlock
Hero headlines, outcome descriptions, before/after stories
Send My Message / Get Started / Join Now
See How It Works / Get [Specific Benefit]
Show Me / Discover / Find Out How
State as fact — back with data
Name the specific thing you do

Pricing pages are among the 3 highest-converting pages on any SaaS site. Yet most pricing pages are vague, confusing, or fail to guide users to the right plan. These specifications directly address that.
H1: Value-led headline ('Simple, Transparent Pricing' or 'Start Free — Grow On Your Terms')
Sub-headline: 1 sentence removing the #1 objection ('No credit card required. Cancel anytime.')
Billing toggle: Monthly / Annual (show annual savings as percentage: 'Save 20%')
Pricing cards: 2–4 plans maximum (more creates paralysis)
'Most Popular' badge: Apply to middle/recommended plan (increases that plan's selection 37%)
Feature comparison table: Full comparison below the cards for detail-oriented buyers
FAQ section: 5–8 pricing-specific questions (refunds, billing cycles, upgrades, team seats)
Social proof / logos: Customer logos or testimonials below FAQ
Short, benefit-oriented: Starter / Pro / Enterprise OR Free / Growth / Scale
Large, prominent number. Strike-through original if discounted. Per month/year clear.
Show Enterprise or top plan FIRST (left) — makes middle plan look reasonable
5–8 features per plan. Use checkmarks (✓) for included, em dash (—) for excluded.
Full-width, high-contrast. 'Get Started' / 'Start Free Trial' / 'Contact Sales'
Top of recommended card, colored (not gray). Increases selection 20–37%.
'No credit card required' / 'Cancel anytime' / 'Upgrade or downgrade anytime'
3 plans where middle = target. Decoy top plan makes middle look affordable.
Show highest price first (left to right), then target plan, then entry plan.
Default to annual toggle ON — show monthly price but bill annually.
Show per-seat price prominently. '$25/user/month' is more digestible than '$250/month for 10 users'.
Name plans by the outcome they enable, not the features: 'Launch' not 'Basic'.

Tone is how you say something; voice is who is saying it. Your voice stays constant across all content. Your tone shifts based on context — more empathetic in support, more confident in sales copy, more precise in documentation.
Command structure, active voice, strong verbs, direct statements, no hedging
Hero headlines, CTA copy, product descriptions
'Your conversion rate increases from day one.' not 'might increase'
Contractions, conversational, relatable analogies, short sentences
Blog posts, email newsletters, onboarding copy
'Look, SEO is confusing. Here's what actually matters.'
'Organizations implementing this approach report 27% higher conversion rates.'
Acknowledge pain, validate feelings, genuine help, no toxic positivity
Support pages, error messages, apology emails
'We know this is frustrating. Here's exactly what happened and what we're doing.'

Fixes in this version: CSS design tokens (custom properties) added, dark mode CSS added, complete blog post HTML template added, favicon spec added, prefers-reduced-motion added to all animations, @supports fallback added for animation-timeline, og: meta tags added to HTML template.
Core Web Vitals compliant (LCP<2.5s, INP<200ms, CLS<0.1) | Google/Bing schema validated | WCAG 2.1 AA+ compliant | Browser fallbacks included

Without CSS custom properties, changing your teal color requires editing dozens of files. With tokens, you change one line and the entire site updates. Tokens also make dark mode trivial — just redefine the tokens inside a dark-mode media query.
--color-accent: #0D7377; /* Transformative Teal — use this value OR your brand hex */
--color-focus: #0D7377; /* Focus ring — same as accent in light mode */
/* ── MANUAL DARK MODE TOGGLE (add .dark class to <html>) ── */
/* Semantic colors — ALL must be redefined for dark mode: */
--color-focus: #2DCBD4; /* Lighter teal — sufficient contrast on dark bg */
.container { max-width: var(--max-page); margin: 0 auto; padding: 0 var(--space-24); }
font-display: swap; /* Prevents CLS from font loading */
animation-timeline: view() has ~75% global browser support (March 2026). Always include @supports checks and Intersection Observer fallbacks so the remaining 25% of users still see your content.
/* ── STEP 1: Default state (visible if no animation support) ── */
Parallax — Disable on Mobile and Reduced Motion
background-position: center; background-repeat: no-repeat; background-size: cover;
<meta name="description" content="155-160 char compelling description with CTA">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#0D7377"><!-- Mobile browser chrome color -->
<!-- Self-hosted font preload (if using local Inter) — crossorigin REQUIRED even for same-origin -->
<!-- Without crossorigin, browser downloads font twice (preload + CSS fetch) -->
<!-- DARK MODE: Inline script in <head> BEFORE stylesheet — only acceptable render-blocking script -->
<!-- Prevents flash-of-wrong-theme (FOIT). Must run before any CSS is applied. -->
<!-- SKIP LINK: First element in <body> — required for keyboard accessibility (WCAG 2.4.1) -->
1–2 sentence subheadline explaining your value.
<a href="/trial" class="btn-primary mt-32" aria-label="Start your free trial">
alt="Product showing key workflow feature in action"
2–3 sentence description of the feature and its benefit.
How Do You [Achieve Key Outcome]?
What Do Our Customers Say?
1–2 sentences reinforcing value and reducing last friction.
Start My Free Trial
No credit card required · Cancel anytime
6.5 Complete Blog Post HTML Template ✦ NEW
<meta name="description" content="155-160 char description with CTA">
<!-- Font preload — crossorigin REQUIRED even for same-origin fonts -->
<!-- DARK MODE: MUST be in <head> BEFORE stylesheet to prevent flash-of-wrong-theme -->
<!-- SKIP LINK: Required for keyboard accessibility — must be first element in <body> -->
TL;DR: 40–60 word direct answer to the article question.
This appears in the first 540 words for AI grounding. Be direct.
Context paragraph. Primary keyword in first 100 words.
Second intro paragraph expanding on the problem.
2–4 paragraphs. Answer directly after H2.
<dd>40–60 word standalone answer. Answers must make sense without context.</dd>
CTA pointing to related content or next step.
A complete favicon setup requires 4 files minimum. Missing favicon = browser shows blank tab icon = looks unfinished and unprofessional.
Modern browsers — scales perfectly at any size
iOS home screen icon when user saves to home screen
Android PWA splash screen, app stores
PWA manifest — references icons, defines app name/color
"lang": "en-US", // ISO 639-1 + region. For multilingual PWAs: one manifest per locale
// For multilingual PWAs: serve different manifest per locale

Pricing pages are among the top 3 highest-converting pages on any SaaS site. The structure below implements every specification from Part 5.9: anchoring, decoy pricing, Most Popular badge, FAQ section, and friction-reducing micro-copy.
<meta name="description" content="Start free. No credit card required. Upgrade or cancel anytime.">
<!-- Font preload — crossorigin REQUIRED even for same-origin fonts -->
<!-- DARK MODE: Inline script in <head> BEFORE stylesheet — prevents flash-of-wrong-theme -->
<!-- SKIP LINK: First element in <body> — required for keyboard accessibility (WCAG 2.4.1) -->
Start free. No credit card required. Cancel anytime.
<!-- BILLING TOGGLE: default annual = saves money for company -->
<!-- SECTION 2: PRICING CARDS (anchoring: Enterprise first, then Pro, then Starter) -->
<!-- PLAN 1: Enterprise (anchor — shown first to make Pro look affordable) -->
<!-- MOBILE NOTE: On mobile, CSS order property reorders to Pro first (see CSS below) -->
For large teams with advanced needs
<!-- PLAN 2: Pro — RECOMMENDED (Most Popular badge increases selection 37%) -->
<!-- PLAN 3: Starter (entry level — priced to make Pro look like best value) -->
// Show "Free" instead of "$0" for free-tier plans
<!-- MOBILE PRICING ORDER: Pro first on small screens (most relevant to typical visitor) -->
<!-- Mobile: Pro → Starter → Enterprise (most relevant first on small screens) -->
<dd>Yes. Upgrade or downgrade at any time. Changes take effect immediately.
When upgrading, you pay the prorated difference for the remaining billing period.</dd>
<dd>Yes. We offer a 30-day money-back guarantee on all paid plans.
Contact support within 30 days of your first charge for a full refund.</dd>
<dd>All major credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, American Express) and PayPal.
Enterprise customers can pay by invoice.</dd>
<dd>Yes. Start with any paid plan free for 14 days.
No credit card required. Full access to all features during your trial.</dd>
<dd>Your account stays active until the end of your billing period.
After that, you move to the free Starter plan automatically.
Your data is retained for 30 days before permanent deletion.</dd>
darkMode: "class", // Enables manual dark mode toggle via .dark class
Performance is the foundation everything else builds on. Sites meeting Core Web Vitals targets see 18% lower bounce rates and 12% higher conversions. Fix added: video embed performance strategy.

These are the three metrics Google uses to measure page experience — each directly impacts rankings and conversion rate. All three must be in the 'Good' column before any other optimisation work is prioritised.

48% of mobile sites, 56% of desktop sites currently achieve good Core Web Vitals. Top 1,000 sites globally: 59% desktop, 51% mobile pass rate. Excellence is achievable but requires dedicated optimization.

Each optimisation below is ranked by its impact on Core Web Vitals. Address them in order — the first items deliver the largest gains for the least effort. Most sites can achieve good CWV by implementing the first 8 items alone.
Enable Gzip or Brotli for all text assets (25–70% size reduction)
1 year for static assets, 1 day for HTML
Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, or Vercel Edge
Audit Network tab — target <50 total requests
Inline critical above-fold CSS, defer non-critical
defer or async on all non-critical scripts
font-display: swap + <link rel='preload'> for critical fonts
Add <link rel='preconnect'> for Google Fonts, CDN, API origins
Tree-shake JS, PurgeCSS for unused CSS
Use CDN, edge functions, server-side caching
Images typically comprise 50–70% of total page weight. Comprehensive image optimization is the single highest-impact performance improvement for most websites.
Icons, logos, simple graphics — never for photos
<!-- NOSCRIPT FALLBACK for users with JavaScript disabled -->
<!-- Required if you use JavaScript-based lazy loading (IntersectionObserver) -->
<!-- Native loading="lazy" works without JS — no noscript needed for native lazy -->
<!-- Hero LCP image: preload in <head>, eager loading, NO lazy -->

YouTube and Vimeo iframes are notorious LCP and INP killers. An unoptimized YouTube embed adds 500KB–1.5MB to your page load and blocks the main thread. NEVER embed YouTube/Vimeo iframes directly. Use a facade pattern instead.
/* Aspect ratio container — prevents layout shift as video loads */
/* Modern CSS approach (no aspect-ratio hack needed in 2026): */
aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; /* Supported in all modern browsers */
<!-- Instead of iframe, show a clickable thumbnail that loads YouTube on click -->
role="button" tabindex="0" aria-label="Play video: Video Title Here">
<!-- Load thumbnail from YouTube (no iframe until click) -->
// KEYBOARD SUPPORT: Activate on Enter or Space (WCAG 2.1.1)
e.preventDefault(); // Prevent page scroll on Space
/* Result: 0KB on initial load vs 500KB-1.5MB for raw YouTube iframe */
<5MB for ambient/background video, <50MB for feature demo
Muted autoplay only — never autoplay with sound
Browsers block unmuted autoplay; users hate it
Prevents blank area while video loads
'auto' causes unnecessary data usage on mobile
<!-- Complete <video> element with all required attributes -->
poster="/images/video-poster.webp" <!-- REQUIRED: prevents blank area on load -->
preload="metadata" <!-- metadata = load duration only. Never "auto". -->
<!-- CAPTIONS: REQUIRED by WCAG 1.2.2 for all pre-recorded video with dialogue -->
Your browser does not support HTML video.
aria-hidden="true"> <!-- aria-hidden: purely decorative, screen readers ignore -->
<!-- .vtt caption file format example (WebVTT) — save as captions-en.vtt: -->
<!-- NOTE: WebVTT files use NO comment markers. Plain text only. -->
Here you can see the main dashboard.

Framework and service choices made at the start of a project determine the performance ceiling. The table below shows which stack delivers the best Core Web Vitals by default, before any optimisation work.
Server components, automatic code splitting, edge runtime
Best in class — 0 JS by default
Zero JavaScript shipped unless component needs it
Framework-defined infrastructure, instant deployment, Stripe Black Friday microsite in 19 days
Execute JS at edge for sub-100ms response times worldwide
Row Level Security, real-time, rapid development standard
React Email templates, developer-friendly, reliable infrastructure
Auto WebP/AVIF conversion, global delivery, resize on demand
All three CWV, LCP element identification
Core Web Vitals report, coverage issues
Full audit: performance, accessibility, SEO, best practices

The median conversion rate across industries is 6.6%. Top performers achieve 15–35% through systematic testing. Fix added: complete A/B testing framework with tools, methodology, and statistical significance guidance.
Users decide in 3–5 seconds. If any of the 5 elements below is missing, you are losing visitors who could have converted.
6–10 words explaining exactly what you do for exactly who
Users scan, not read. 6 words land before they blink.
One clear action, prominently placed, high contrast color
One decision = easy. Multiple CTAs = paralysis = no click.
Customer logos, user count, or testimonials in first viewport
Trust reduces hesitation. Show the win before explaining the how.
Real product screenshot or genuine usage — not stock photography
Users want to see what they're buying. Stock destroys credibility.
User must know exactly what clicking CTA does
Ambiguity = doubt = abandonment. Be specific about the next step.
Button size and copy are the two highest-leverage variables on any page. Both must be correct simultaneously — a perfectly worded CTA on a too-small button still fails on mobile.
High contrast with background, brand accent
Never gray — use primary accent color
Color shift, shadow, or scale — clear visual feedback
Above fold + repeated throughout page at decision points
Capture users at different stages of consideration
Removes the #1 objection before they voice it
Trust is the single biggest conversion lever that most sites underinvest in. Every element below removes a specific objection. Missing even one leaves a segment of potential customers unconverted.
Real people, real names, real photos — include company and role
SSL seal, payment security logos, compliance certs near forms
Exact counts: '10,000+ teams' — avoid vague 'thousands of users'
Specificity builds trust; vague claims don't
'42 people viewing now' / 'Last purchased 3 hours ago'
Clear, prominent, hassle-free — near primary CTA
Reduces perceived risk, increases trial starts
Below primary CTA: 'No credit card required — cancel anytime'
Recognized customer logos in social proof bar above fold
Borrowed authority — if they trust you, I can too
'47% increase in conversions in 30 days' — quantified outcomes
Specific data converts; vague promises don't
Forms are the final step before conversion — the point where the most abandonment happens. Every extra field, unclear label, or generic error message is friction that costs you a customer.
Only ask for what you need right now — get more later
Target 3–4 fields maximum for first conversion
Always visible labels above fields — never placeholder-only
Inline validation with specific, helpful error messages
Vague errors cause abandonment — tell exactly what to fix
Show progress bar + step count for multi-step forms
Security badge or trust statement adjacent to submit button
IMB Bank: increased completion by highlighting trust near form
Specific benefit: 'Get My Free Report' not 'Submit'
'Submit' is the single weakest CTA text in existence
Use type='email', type='tel', type='number' for correct keyboard
Wrong keyboard = immediate friction on mobile

Assumptions about what converts are usually wrong. A/B testing replaces guesswork with data. Small 5–10% improvements compound: 5% better CTA + 5% better headline + 5% better layout = ~16% total improvement.
Form a hypothesis: 'Changing CTA from [X] to [Y] will increase clicks because [reason]'
Identify the metric: Primary (conversion rate), Secondary (click rate, bounce rate)
Calculate sample size: Use an A/B sample size calculator before starting (e.g., Evan Miller's calculator at evanmiller.org/ab-testing/sample-size.html)
Minimum visitors: 1,000 per variation before evaluating results
Minimum runtime: 2 full business weeks (captures weekly cycles)
Statistical significance: 95% confidence interval required before declaring winner
One variable at a time: Never test multiple changes simultaneously (use multivariate for that)
Document results: Log every test, hypothesis, result, and next action in a testing log
Testing Priority Order (Highest to Lowest Impact)
Test brand accent vs high-contrast alternative
Above fold vs below hero vs both
Hero image (product vs lifestyle vs abstract)
Plan count, anchoring order, 'Most Popular' placement
Depends heavily on audience and offer
Before declaring an A/B test winner, the result must meet the 95% confidence threshold. This means there is only a 5% chance the result is due to random variation rather than the actual change.
Testing a CTA (5% conversion rate, 10% relative lift)
Testing a headline (1% click rate, 20% relative lift)
Testing a pricing page (3% conversion, 15% relative lift)
Testing a form (8% submission rate, 15% relative lift)

These principles exploit how humans make decisions — not rationally, but emotionally and cognitively. Apply them ethically: only use scarcity and urgency when they are genuine. Manufactured urgency destroys trust permanently.
'Offer expires in 24 hours' — only genuine urgency
Show highest price first, then target plan
Enterprise → Pro → Starter makes Pro seem fair
Frame around what they lose by NOT acting
'Don't lose another lead' beats 'Gain more leads'
Provide genuine value upfront before asking
'Sarah M. from Notion' beats 'a happy customer'
Small yes → medium yes → large yes
Email quiz → result → small offer → full offer
Real-world results from published case studies. Use these as benchmarks when setting A/B test targets and prioritising which elements to optimise first.
Homepage redesign based on heatmap analysis

This entire part addresses gaps that were completely absent from all 9 source documents. Security and legal requirements are not optional — they are legal obligations in most jurisdictions and production deployment requirements for any serious website.
Security headers are HTTP response headers that protect users from common attacks. Add these at the server/CDN layer (Cloudflare, Vercel headers config, nginx, or Apache).
// NOTE: X-XSS-Protection is DEPRECATED and removed from this config.
// OWASP explicitly recommends NOT using it (can introduce XSS vulnerabilities in IE).
// Use Content-Security-Policy instead for XSS protection.
"value":"default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com;"}
// The CSP above uses unsafe-inline for script-src and style-src.
// This is a STARTING POINT ONLY. unsafe-inline largely defeats XSS protection
// because it allows inline scripts — exactly what XSS attacks inject.
// PRODUCTION-GRADE CSP: Replace unsafe-inline with nonces or hashes.
// Option A — Nonce-based (recommended for dynamic pages):
// Generate a fresh nonce on each server render and inject it into
// Option B — Hash-based (for static inline scripts):
// Generate hash: echo -n "your script" | openssl dgst -sha256 -binary | openssl base64
// Easiest path: Use a CSP evaluator: csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com
Forces HTTPS — prevents SSL stripping attacks
Prevents clickjacking (embedding your site in an iframe)
Whitelist allowed sources (see nonce/hash guidance above)
Prevents XSS — this is the correct replacement for X-XSS-Protection
Controls what URL info is sent to other sites
Prevents sites accessing device hardware without consent
OWASP recommends removing this header. It was deprecated by Chrome/Firefox and can introduce XSS vulnerabilities in IE. Use CSP instead.

Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks trick users into submitting forms on your site from a malicious page. Every form that changes state (login, signup, password change, purchase) must be protected.
<!-- Method 3: Double-submit cookie pattern for APIs -->
// Include X-CSRF-Token header in AJAX requests
<!-- Hidden honeypot field — bots fill it, humans don't see it -->
<!-- Honeypot: hidden from humans via CSS, bots fill it -->
// Server-side: reject submissions where "website" field is not empty
Without rate limiting, contact forms and signup endpoints can be abused by bots. Three implementation options — choose based on your stack:
// OPTION 1: Cloudflare Rate Limiting (any stack — recommended)
// Dashboard: Security → WAF → Rate limiting rules
// Rule: path=/api/submit, requests>5/minute per IP → Block 1 hour
// No code changes required — handled at network edge
max: 5, // 5 requests per minute per IP
message: { error: "Too many submissions. Please try again later." },
const ratelimit = new Ratelimit({ redis: Redis.fromEnv(),
if (!success) return new Response("Too many requests", { status: 429 });
Login and authentication pages are the highest-risk attack surface on any website. A breach exposes all user data. These are non-negotiable security requirements for any site with user accounts.
Use bcrypt (cost factor 12+) or Argon2id — NEVER store plain text or MD5/SHA1
MD5/SHA1 hashed passwords can be cracked in seconds. bcrypt is time-expensive by design.
Min 8 characters. Allow all characters. Check against HaveIBeenPwned breach database.
NIST SP 800-63B guidelines — complexity rules are less effective than length + breach checking.
Lock account after 5–10 failed attempts OR implement progressive delays (exponential backoff)
Prevents brute-force attacks. Use CAPTCHA after 3 failures as softer alternative.
HTTP-only cookies, Secure flag, SameSite=Strict, 30-day expiry maximum
HTTP-only prevents JS access (XSS-safe). Secure forces HTTPS-only transmission.
Offer TOTP (Google Authenticator/Authy) or passkeys — make MFA easy to enable
MFA blocks 99.9% of account compromise attacks (Microsoft data, 2023).
Time-limited (15 min) single-use tokens — invalidate on use OR on new login
Persistent reset links are a common account takeover vector.
Use proven auth libraries: NextAuth.js, Auth.js, Supabase Auth, Clerk, Auth0
Don't build authentication from scratch — use battle-tested implementations.
Beautiful pre-built UI components, passkeys, MFA built-in
Integrated with database RLS, magic links, social login
Open source, zero vendor lock-in, 50+ OAuth providers

GDPR (EU), UK GDPR, CCPA (California), LGPD (Brazil), PDPA (India/Thailand), and PIPL (China) all require user consent before setting tracking cookies or processing personal data. Non-compliance fines: up to €20M or 4% of global annual turnover (GDPR). This is not optional.
Session cookies, CSRF tokens, shopping cart, dark mode preference
Google Analytics, Hotjar, Microsoft Clarity, Plausible (with IP anonymisation)
Facebook Pixel, Google Ads, LinkedIn Insight Tag
Saved user preferences with PII, personalisation features
YES if tied to personal data
Building a compliant cookie consent system from scratch is complex. Use a proven library that handles the legal requirements automatically:
We use cookies to analyze traffic. See our Privacy Policy.
// Load analytics only if user has consented
// Google Analytics example — swap for your analytics script
// WCAG 2.1.2: Modal dialogs must be keyboard-dismissible via Escape key
// ⚠ IMPORTANT: For GDPR compliance you must also:
// 1. Block ALL third-party tracking scripts on page load (before consent)
// 2. Provide a way to withdraw consent (link in footer: "Cookie Preferences")
// 3. List all cookies in your Cookie Policy page
// 4. Re-request consent if cookie policy changes significantly
What data you collect, how you use it, user rights, contact details, cookie policy
Acceptable use, intellectual property, limitation of liability, governing law
GDPR (EU) mandatory if using non-essential cookies
List of all cookies, purpose, duration, opt-out mechanism
Form to request data export, deletion, or correction
<!-- Footer must include these links on every page -->
© 2026 Your Company Name. All rights reserved.

WCAG 2.1 Level AA is the legal standard required by the ADA (USA), EN 301 549 (EU), and equivalent laws in UK, Canada, Australia, and India. WCAG AAA is recommended for body text. This document requires both.
Alt text for all images | Captions for video | Color not used alone to convey info | 4.5:1 contrast AA / 7:1 AAA
on every img | in
All functionality keyboard accessible | No keyboard traps | Skip navigation link | Focus indicators visible | No content flashing >3 times/sec
Test with Tab key only | Add :focus-visible styles | Add Skip to content at top of body
Language declared | Error suggestions in forms | Consistent navigation | Labels associated with inputs
<html lang='en'> | <label for='inputId'> | Aria-describedby for error messages
Valid HTML | ARIA roles used correctly | Name/role/value for all UI components
Run W3C HTML Validator | Test with NVDA (Windows) or VoiceOver (Mac/iOS)

Users with vestibular disorders, epilepsy, or migraines can be physically harmed by web animations. WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3 requires providing a mechanism to disable motion. The simplest implementation is a CSS media query that disables all animations for users who have enabled 'Reduce Motion' in their OS settings.
* This removes ALL animations and transitions for users who
* have enabled "Reduce Motion" in their OS settings.
* macOS: System Preferences > Accessibility > Display > Reduce Motion
* iOS: Settings > Accessibility > Motion > Reduce Motion
* Windows: Settings > Ease of Access > Display > Show animations
/* Never use outline: none without a custom alternative */
/* Remove focus ring for mouse users (but keep for keyboard users) */
/* Skip navigation link (keyboard / screen reader essential) */
No single tool catches all accessibility issues. Use automated tools first for quick wins, then manual testing to catch what automation misses. WCAG compliance requires both.
~35% of WCAG issues — fast scan of any page
Color contrast, missing alt text, form labels, structure
Runs on every page — aim for 90+ score
Test keyboard navigation and screen reader experience
Test on Apple devices — Cmd+F5 to activate on Mac
Tab through every interactive element on every page
404 pages have real conversion and brand expression value. A well-designed 404 can recover 10–30% of users who would otherwise leave. A bare 404 loses them permanently.
Clear heading: '404 — Page Not Found' (tell users what happened)
Human explanation: 1–2 sentences — 'This page may have moved, been renamed, or no longer exists.'
Search bar: Let users search for what they were looking for
Popular/top pages: 3–5 links to your most visited pages
Navigation: Same header as the rest of the site (don't orphan users)
Brand personality: Use tone and design consistent with the rest of your site
NO auto-redirect: Never auto-redirect from 404 to homepage — it confuses users and hurts SEO
Return HTTP 404 status code: Ensure the server returns actual 404, not 200 with a 'not found' page

Two complete workflow guides: a 10-step process for building a single webpage, a 6-phase launch plan, the complete 40-item pre-launch checklist, and — new in this version — a post-launch ongoing cadence calendar.
10.1 How to Build a Complete Webpage (10 Steps)
~12 hours for a complete, production-ready, optimized webpage.
Sketch: Header → Hero → Features → Testimonials → CTA → Footer
Mark image locations: hero right, feature sections, testimonials
Identify scroll effect candidates: fade-in on cards/images, parallax on hero only
List all content needed: headlines, descriptions, CTAs, social proof
STEP 2: Set Up HTML Structure (1 hr)
Add ONE H1 at the top — mandatory, no exceptions
Structure each section with H2 as searchable questions
Add skip navigation link as first element in <body>
Place image placeholders with descriptive alt text
Set CSS tokens (Part 6.1 :root block) — do this FIRST
Set all color tokens in :root (light mode)
Set all dark mode token overrides in @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)
Text: #1a1a1a (near black) on Cloud Dancer (#F5F5F0) backgrounds
Test every text/background combination: 7:1 ratio minimum
STEP 5: Add Head Meta & Schema (30 min)
Canonical tag, title tag (50–60 chars), meta description (155–160 chars)
Open Graph: og:title, og:description, og:image (1200×630px), og:url
meta name='theme-color' for mobile browser chrome color
Add loading='lazy' to all below-fold images
Alt text on every image (10–15 descriptive words)
Create OG image: 1200×630px JPEG for social sharing
Add CSS tokens and @supports fallback before any animation code
Add @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) block — disable all animations
Add .fade-in class to content images and cards
Add parallax to hero background only (max 2 sections)
Add Intersection Observer JavaScript fallback for older browsers
Test on real mobile device — reduce or remove effects if janky
Stack hero on mobile: image below text
Touch targets: 44px minimum on all interactive elements
Test on real device — not just browser resize
Add security headers (vercel.json or nginx config — see Part 9.1)
Implement cookie consent banner (load analytics only after consent)
Verify Privacy Policy, Terms of Service, Cookie Policy pages exist
CSRF protection on all POST endpoints
Add footer legal links: Privacy, Terms, Cookies, Cookie Preferences
STEP 10: Performance Testing & Final QA (1 hr)
Run PageSpeed Insights: Target LCP <2.5s, INP <200ms, CLS <0.1
Check Network tab: <50 requests, <1MB total page weight
Test keyboard-only navigation through every interactive element
Test dark mode: toggle both manually and via OS system preference
Validate schema with Google Rich Results Test
Test on real mobile device (iPhone and Android if possible)
Test all CTAs, links, and form submissions
Check all images have alt text
10.2 Full Website Launch Plan (6 Phases, 10 Weeks)

This plan sequences every action in this system into an order that minimises rework. Phase 1 must complete before Phase 2 begins — skipping phases creates dependencies that cause expensive fixes later.
Set up CSS tokens (Part 6.1) | Implement typography system | Set up color palette (light + dark) | Build spacing grid | Copy-paste utility classes
Typography, colors, layout all implemented with dark mode support
Implement 15-element blog template | Set up HTML page templates (homepage + blog) | Implement favicon and meta tags | Set up OG images
Complete page templates live, first 3 posts published
Write all headlines using Part 5 formulas | Apply CTA formula to all buttons | Write value propositions | Create pricing page
All copy written, pricing page live
Implement all schema markup | Set up canonical tags | Create robots.txt + sitemap.xml | Submit to Search Console & Bing | Set up AEO structure (TL;DRs, FAQs)
SEO fully implemented, submitted to search engines
Implement security headers | Set up cookie consent | Create privacy/terms/cookie pages | Run and fix performance audit | Test accessibility
Security headers live, legal pages published, CWV targets met
Final 40-item pre-launch checklist | Set up analytics (GA4 + Search Console) | Set up RUM monitoring | Establish baseline metrics | Go live
Website launched, baseline metrics recorded, monitoring active
Line height 1.5–1.6 for body, 1.1–1.3 for headings
ONE H1 per page — logical H1→H2→H3→H4 hierarchy
WCAG AAA contrast (7:1) tested for body text — AA (4.5:1) minimum everywhere
Touch targets 44×44px minimum on all mobile elements
Dark mode tested (via OS preference AND manual toggle if implemented)
H1 is a natural question (50–60 characters)
TL;DR summary at top (40–60 words in highlighted box)
Table of contents for all posts >1,000 words
FAQ section with 5–10 questions (40–60 words each answer)
Named author with credentials (15 = Conclusion, 15b = Author Bio separate)
Article or Organization schema in <head> of every relevant page
FAQPage schema for all FAQ sections
BreadcrumbList schema on all interior pages
og:title, og:image (1200×630px), og:description, og:url on every page
twitter:card, twitter:title, twitter:image on every page
Title tag 50–60 chars | Meta description 155–160 chars
Alt text on every image (10–15 descriptive words)
robots.txt present at /robots.txt with Sitemap reference
sitemap.xml submitted to Google Search Console + Bing Webmaster Tools
All images: WebP/AVIF, responsive srcset, lazy loading (except hero)
CSS/JS/HTML all minified | Gzip or Brotli compression enabled
Security headers configured (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) — verify at securityheaders.com
Honeypot fields added to all forms
Cookie consent banner implemented — analytics not loading before consent
Privacy Policy, Terms of Service, Cookie Policy pages all live
GDPR Data Request page (/data-request) live — required under GDPR Articles 15–22
Footer links: Privacy, Terms, Cookies, Cookie Preferences on every page
CSRF protection on all POST form endpoints
Skip navigation link as first element in <body>
:focus-visible styles defined — never outline: none without replacement
Lighthouse Accessibility score 90+ on all key pages
Tested with keyboard-only navigation through every interactive element
All form inputs have associated <label> elements
The 6-phase plan ends at launch. But websites decay without maintenance. This cadence ensures your site improves continuously after launch rather than slowly degrading.
Fix broken internal links and redirects
Identify pages with high impressions/low CTR for headline testing
Full PageSpeed Insights audit on top 5 pages
Identify lowest-converting pages for CRO focus
Screaming Frog (free up to 500 URLs) or Ahrefs Site Audit
Freshness signal for SEO and AEO
Full SEO audit: rankings, backlinks, keyword gaps
Identify new ranking opportunities and content gaps
A/B test review: document results, plan next test
Security audit: review headers, update dependencies
Catch vulnerabilities before they become incidents
Ensure new content hasn't introduced accessibility regressions
Full content audit: update, merge, or remove old posts
Remove thin content, update outdated data, fix cannibalization
Design system review: update tokens for new brand direction
Single-file update propagates across entire site
For content-first businesses with dedicated writers
Optimal for most businesses with 1–2 writers
Only viable if posts are exceptionally deep (4,000+ words)
Not enough to build meaningful organic presence
All 9 source documents merged + 30 identified gaps resolved + 4 new parts added.

Security/legal, dark mode, video performance, A/B testing, pricing pages, post-launch cadence, canonical tags, OG tags, sitemap, robots.txt, H3–H6 mobile specs, prefers-reduced-motion, CSS tokens, favicon, and blog post HTML template are all now included.
